content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement . . . Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5901        322-6441        322-6444

February 27, 1978

John Mahan
Councilman
City of Santa Clara
City Hall
1500 Warburton Avenue
Santa Clara, California 95050

Dear Mr. Mahan:

Thank you for your letter of January 20, 1978, requesting
advice from the Commission with respect to the reporting and
disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act. The
advice set forth below is issued pursuant to Government Code
Section 83114(b).

The facts as I understand them are as follows. A member
of your immediate family wishes to convey to you, as a gift, a
parcel of real property immediately adjacent to the University
Redevelopment Project area in the Old Quad area of Santa Clara.
The property consists of a home built near the turn of the
century which has served as a family home since 1941. You
also stated that even if no present gift is made, you will
probably receive the property by will or intestate succession.
The property has long been located in the CD (Commercial-
Development) zoning district. You are unaware of its fair
market value.

The Redevelopment Project area has been certified as sub-
stantially complete by the Redevelopment Agency. Approximately
7 acres of the area remains undeveloped and will be held by
the City of Santa Clara as surplus property, subject to the
requirements of the Redevelopment Plan. Potential developers
will submit development proposals for this land to the City
Council which will then select a developer for the property.
As required by the Redevelopment Plan, the design and construc-
tion of the development are subject to the approval of the
City Council.

John Mahan
February 27, 1978
Page Two


Based on the above facts, you have requested advice with
respect to your obligations under the conflict of interest pro-
visions of the Political Reform Act.

The basic disqualification provision of the Act is contained
in Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103. Section 87100
provides that:

No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

The term "financial interest" is defined in Section 87103 to
include an interest in real property if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally on the
real property.1/ Thus before you will be disqualified, you
must (1) have an interest in real property; (2) it must be
foreseeable to the decision in question will affect that
interest; (3) the effect must be material; and (4) the effect
must be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.

In order to have an interest in real property, you must
have a present possessory or beneficial interest, a security
interest, a future interest under a trust or a legally enforceable right to property. The mere expectancy pursuant to a
contemplated gift, a will, or intestate secession is not sufficient to create an interest in real property in the hands of a
prospective recipient. If you do not have the requisite interest
in the property, you need not disqualify yourself from participating in decisions which would foreseeably and materially affect
the property.

However, assuming that you have the requisite interest in
the property, and that it is foreseeable that the property will

_________________________

1/ Section 87103(c) also defines financial interest to
include a source of income. In the instant case, I am assuming
that the donor of the gift would not be a source of income to
you because the definition of income excludes gifts received
from close relatives. Section 82030(b)(5).

John Mahan
February 27, 1978
Page Three


be materially affected2/ by a decision, it appears that your
financial interest may not be affected in a manner different
from the effect on the public generally. Commission Regulation,
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703, provides that a decision will
not trigger disqualification if it affects all members of the
public generally or a significant segment thereof substantially the same manner. In the Opinion requested by William
Owen, 2 FPPC Ops. 77 (No. 77-005, June 2, 1976), the Commission
determined that residential homeowners within and in the immediate vicinity of a "core area" of Davis constituted a
significant segment of the public. Thus, if the residence
in question would be affected in substantially the same manner
as all other residences in the vicinity of the redevelopment
area, you would not be disqualified from participating in
decisions regarding the surplus land. I have enclosed two
opinions which may interest you in applying the principal
surrounding the public generally to your case.

I trust this answers your question. If you have any further
questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim
Chief, Conflicts of
Interest Division

TP:mfa
Enclosures


2/ From the facts provided, we are unable to determine
whether decisions regarding the surplus property would foreseeably and materially affect your interest in real property.
I have enclosed Regulation 18702 in order to assist you in
determining whether the property will be materially affected.

THE CITY OF SANTA CLARA
CALIFORNIA

CITY ATTORNEY
CITY HALL
1500 WARBURTON AVE
SANTA CLARA, CA. 95050
(408) 984-3232

January 20, 1978

Fair Political Practices Commission
926 "J" Street, Suite 522
Sacramento, California 95814

Gentlemen:

I hereby respectfully request written advice from the Fair
Political Practices Commission for guidance in my official
activities based on the following facts which I believe are
material:

MATERIAL FACTS

A member of my immediate family wishes to convey to me, as a
gift, a parcel of real property immediately adjacent to the
University Redevelopment Project area in the Old Quad area of
Santa Clara. The property is now, and has since 1941, served as
a family home, and consists of a Victorian-style house built at
or near the turn of the century, but has long been located in a
CD (Commercial-Downtown) zoning district. No current appraisal
of the property has been done, and because of the unique
architecture and location of the house, the current market value
is unknown.

The Redevelopment Project has been certified as substantially
complete by the Redevelopment Agency. The remaining undeveloped
portion of the area, being slightly over 7 acres, will be sold by the
City of Santa Clara as surplus property, subject to the requirements
of the Redevelopment Plan. Proposals for development of this land
will be presented to the City Council, which will then decide to
which developer the land will be sold. Pursuant to the Redevelopment Plan, the design and construction of the development are
subject to the approval of the City Council.

Even if no present gift is made, there exists a strong likelihood
that I will eventually succeed to ownership of the property by
will or intestate succession.

REQUESTED OPINION

In accordance with Government Code § 83114(b) as implemented by your
regulation 18329, I respectfully request that the Fair Political
Practices Commission provide written advice with respect to my

Fair Political Practices

-2-

January 20, 1978

Conflict of Interest duties in connection with City Council
consideration of proposals for final development of the Redevelopment
Project Area. If there are any additional facts or other material
you might desire, please contact me so I can make them available
to you. I would also appreciate your expeditious handling of this
request so that I will have the benefit of the decision for use
with regard to the proposals.

Very truly yours,

John L Mahan

JOHN MAHAN
City Councilman

bd

sections.question: s, please do not hesitate to contact me.
sections.conclusion: The basic disqualification provision requires: (1) an interest in real property; (2) foreseeability that the decision will affect that interest; (3) materiality of the effect; and (4) distinguishability from effects on the public generally. A mere expectancy from a contemplated gift or inheritance is insufficient to create an interest requiring disqualification. If the residence would be affected in substantially the same manner as other residences in the vicinity, disqualification would not be required under Commission Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.
sections.question_synthetic: A Santa Clara city councilman seeks guidance on his conflict of interest obligations regarding participation in city council decisions about a redevelopment project area, given that he expects to receive a family property adjacent to the project area either as a gift or through inheritance.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The councilman is not disqualified based on a mere expectancy of receiving the property through gift or inheritance, as such future interests do not constitute sufficient financial interests under the Political Reform Act. Disqualification would only apply if the redevelopment decisions would materially and distinctly affect his property differently from other residential properties in the area.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (dated January 20, 1978) and FPPC response letter (dated February 27, 1978). Minor OCR errors present but content is generally clear. Date discrepancy noted: response header shows February 27, 1972 but document ID and context indicate 1978. Extracted FPPC response sections per instructions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: s, please do not hesitate to contact me.

CONCLUSION: The basic disqualification provision requires: (1) an interest in real property; (2) foreseeability that the decision will affect that interest; (3) materiality of the effect; and (4) distinguishability from effects on the public generally. A mere expectancy from a contemplated gift or inheritance is insufficient to create an interest requiring disqualification. If the residence would be affected in substantially the same manner as other residences in the vicinity, disqualification would not be required under Commission Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Councilman John Mahan of Santa Clara that he is not disqualified from participating in redevelopment project decisions based on his expected inheritance or gift of adjacent property, since mere expectancies do not constitute financial interests under the Political Reform Act, and any effects would likely be felt by all neighboring homeowners similarly.
